# Executive Technology Brief

**Date:** 2026-07-19

**Execution Time:** 03:00 AM (Local Company Time)

**Report ID:** ETB-2026-07-19-001

**Generated By:**
Technology Intelligence Routine

*Coverage note: this edition covers July 13–19, 2026, building on ETB-2026-07-18. The dominant story is a hardening consensus that AI infrastructure spending is now cannibalizing traditional IT budgets and reshaping market leadership — IBM suffered its worst single-day stock drop on record after warning that enterprise clients are redirecting capex toward AI hardware, and two days later Apple retook the title of world's most valuable company from Nvidia as investors rotated away from AI-infrastructure pure-plays. Big Tech's Q2 earnings season (July 22–30) will be the next test of whether ~$725B in combined hyperscaler AI capex is converting to revenue. Separately, "AI agent" is now consolidating into its own risk and governance category on three fronts at once: security (a second, distinct npm campaign is now stealing AI coding-assistant session data specifically, not just generic credentials), standards (a Google-led coalition of eleven companies published a rival agent-discovery spec that pointedly excludes Anthropic and OpenAI from its steering group), and regulation (China's agent-specific rules took effect July 15, and Illinois became the first US state to mandate frontier-model safety audits).*

---

# 1. Executive Snapshot

- **AI CAPEX IS NOW VISIBLY REPRICING THE MARKET, NOT JUST FUNDING IT.** IBM's stock fell 25% in a single day (July 14, its worst day on record) after warning that enterprise clients abruptly redirected spend from IBM software/infrastructure toward AI hardware; three days later, Apple overtook Nvidia as the world's most valuable company as investors grew skeptical of AI-infrastructure spending pace. Big Tech's Q2 earnings (July 22–30) will show whether ~$725B in 2026 hyperscaler AI capex is converting to revenue — a leading indicator for vendor pricing stability and contract terms over the next 12 months. **Attention level: High — treat as a live input for any vendor-negotiation or long-term infrastructure commitment made this quarter.**

- **A NEW NPM SUPPLY-CHAIN CAMPAIGN IS DELIBERATELY TARGETING AI CODING-ASSISTANT SESSION DATA — NOT JUST GENERIC CREDENTIALS.** A compromised `jscrambler` npm package (July 11) shipped a hidden infostealer that specifically harvests Cursor/Claude Code-style session data alongside cloud credentials and crypto wallets — a distinct campaign from the "Miasma" incident tracked in yesterday's brief, confirming attackers now treat developer AI-assistant sessions as a first-class target, not an incidental one. **Attention level: High — audit CI/CD pipelines and developer machines for both campaigns, and treat AI-assistant session tokens as credentials requiring the same rotation discipline as API keys.**

- **TWO CRITICAL, ACTIVELY-EXPLOITED VULNERABILITIES HAVE FEDERAL DEADLINES THAT HAVE ALREADY PASSED.** SonicWall SMA1000 (CVE-2026-15409, CVSS 10.0, unauthenticated SSRF chained with command injection for full appliance takeover; federal deadline was July 17) and an Active Directory Federation Services privilege-escalation flaw (CVE-2026-56155) are both confirmed under active exploitation. Any company running SonicWall SMA1000 for remote-access VPN or on-prem AD FS for SSO should treat this as unresolved, not historical. **Attention level: High — verify patch status today regardless of whether the deadline already passed.**

- **THE AGENT ECOSYSTEM IS FRACTURING INTO COMPETING STANDARDS AND REGULATORY REGIMES SIMULTANEOUSLY.** A Google-led coalition (Microsoft, GitHub, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Snowflake, Databricks, Cisco, NVIDIA, Hugging Face) published the "Agentic Resource Discovery" spec for agent/tool discovery — notable for organizing around, and pointedly outside, Anthropic and OpenAI. In parallel, China's agent-specific regulatory regime took effect July 15 and Illinois became the first US state to mandate third-party safety audits for frontier developers. Companies building multi-agent stacks now face both a protocol choice and a compliance surface that didn't exist a month ago. **Attention level: Medium — do not commit to deep agent-tooling investment until the standards question settles further, but start tracking compliance exposure now.**

- **CAPITAL IS CONCENTRATING SHARPLY IN VERTICAL AI AND ALTERNATIVE INFERENCE INFRASTRUCTURE, SIGNALING FRONTIER-MODEL LOCK-IN IS BECOMING A LIVE STRATEGIC CONCERN FOR THE MARKET, NOT JUST THIS OFFICE.** Fireworks AI raised $1.5B (Series D, $17.5B valuation, $1B+ ARR) explicitly pitching itself as an alternative to relying solely on frontier labs; Chai Discovery raised $400M for vertical (life-sciences) AI; AI dev-tools funding hit $2.0B YTD, up 85% YoY, concentrated almost entirely in agentic coding platforms. **Attention level: Medium — an opportunity signal for vendor diversification, not an immediate action.**

# 2. Technologies Worth Watching

---

### Technology 1: IBM's Record Stock Crash and the Q2 Earnings Test for AI Capex ROI

**Category:** Enterprise Solutions / Cloud & Infrastructure / Global Technology Direction

**What happened?**
IBM shares fell 25% on July 14 — the worst single-day drop in the company's history — after warning Q2 revenue and EPS would miss consensus, with CEO Arvind Krishna attributing it to enterprise clients abruptly redirecting capex from IBM software/infrastructure toward AI hardware (memory chips, servers) in late June, causing large deals to slip. Big Tech's Q2 earnings season opens July 22 (Alphabet), with Microsoft, Meta (July 29), Amazon, and Apple (July 30) to follow; Goldman Sachs estimates the four hyperscalers will spend ~$725B combined on AI infrastructure this year.

**Why it matters?**
This is the first concrete evidence that enterprise AI infrastructure spending is actively cannibalizing traditional IT/software budgets, not simply adding to them — a leading indicator for how vendors' pricing power and contract flexibility may shift over the next two quarters. The Q2 earnings window is the next checkpoint for whether that capex is converting to usable revenue or building toward a correction.

**Business Value:** (1) Do not treat current incumbent-vendor pricing and support terms as stable through year-end — use IBM's warning as leverage in any renewal negotiation happening this quarter. (2) Watch the July 22–30 earnings window specifically for Azure/AWS/Google Cloud growth-rate commentary, which will be the clearest signal of whether AI capex is translating to durable revenue or overbuild. (3) If evaluating new long-term infrastructure commitments, favor shorter contract terms or renegotiation clauses until this capex-ROI question resolves.

**Company Relevance:** 55 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Observe (through Q2 earnings), then Pilot (renegotiate vendor terms if signals confirm budget instability).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (earnings window) informing Medium-term vendor strategy.

---

### Technology 2: Jscrambler npm Supply-Chain Attack — AI Coding-Assistant Sessions as a Named Target

**Category:** Open Source Ecosystem / Developer Ecosystem / Security

**What happened?**
On July 11, attackers used stolen publisher credentials to push malicious versions of the widely-used `jscrambler` npm package and its plugins, embedding a hidden Rust-based infostealer that specifically harvests cloud credentials, crypto wallets, browser data, and — notably — AI coding-assistant (Cursor/Claude Code-style) session data. Socket.dev detected the compromise within six minutes; roughly 1,479 downloads occurred before removal. This is a distinct campaign from the "Miasma" attack (AsyncAPI, Red Hat) tracked in yesterday's brief.

**Why it matters?**
Two unrelated npm supply-chain campaigns within a week, both targeting developer tooling, with this one explicitly naming AI-assistant session data as a harvest target, confirms that developer AI-assistant credentials are now a recognized, valuable attack surface in their own right — not an incidental byproduct of a broader credential-theft campaign.

**Business Value:** (1) Treat AI coding-assistant session tokens (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, etc.) with the same rotation and storage discipline as API keys and cloud credentials — most teams currently do not. (2) Audit whether `jscrambler` or its plugins are anywhere in the dependency tree (direct or transitive) and rotate any exposed secrets if so. (3) Expand the npm/CI provenance audit already recommended in yesterday's brief (for Miasma) to explicitly check for AI-assistant-targeting infostealers — this is now a confirmed, repeatable attack category, not a single incident.

**Company Relevance:** 55 / 100 (higher for any engineering org using AI coding assistants in CI or on developer machines — which is nearly all of them per the adoption data below)

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Adopt (audit dependencies and rotate exposed AI-assistant session credentials immediately).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (audit); Long-term (establishes AI-assistant credentials as a standing security category).

---

### Technology 3: SonicWall SMA1000 Zero-Days — Federal Deadline Already Passed, Actively Chained

**Category:** Cloud & Infrastructure / Security

**What happened?**
CVE-2026-15409 (CVSS 10.0, unauthenticated SSRF in the SMA1000 Work Place interface) and CVE-2026-15410 (post-auth OS command injection in the Management Console) are being actively chained together for full appliance takeover. Both were added to CISA's KEV catalog July 14 with a federal remediation deadline of July 17 — already past as of this report.

**Why it matters?**
SMA1000 is widely deployed for remote-access VPN, meaning a successful chain gives attackers a direct foothold into internal networks. A passed federal deadline does not mean reduced risk — it means any unpatched instance has now had an extra window of exposure on top of the original disclosure.

**Business Value:** (1) Verify patch status on any SonicWall SMA1000 deployment today — this is not a "monitor" item, it is a "confirm remediation happened" item given the deadline has already passed. (2) If SMA1000 was internet-facing and unpatched during the exposure window, treat it as potentially compromised and audit for lateral movement, not just apply the patch. (3) Cross-reference this with the AD FS privilege-escalation CVE (CVE-2026-56155, also added to KEV July 14) — both are identity/remote-access infrastructure hit in the same window, warranting a combined incident-response check rather than two separate tickets.

**Company Relevance:** 40 / 100 (much higher if SonicWall SMA1000 or on-prem AD FS is part of the remote-access/identity stack)

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Adopt (verify patch/compromise status immediately).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term — deadline has already passed.

---

### Technology 4: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) — A Rival Agent-Interop Standard Emerges Outside Anthropic and OpenAI

**Category:** Artificial Intelligence / Developer Ecosystem

**What happened?**
A coalition led by Google — including Microsoft, GitHub, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Snowflake, Databricks, Cisco, NVIDIA, and Hugging Face — announced the Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) specification, an open standard for publishing, discovering, and verifying AI tools, APIs, and agents. It is positioned as complementary to (not a replacement for) Anthropic's MCP and Google's own A2A protocol, but reporting frames it explicitly as an effort by Google and Microsoft to counterbalance Anthropic and OpenAI's platform leverage. Early adopters include GitHub's Copilot Agent Finder and Hugging Face's Discover Tool.

**Why it matters?**
Multi-agent architectures depend on a stable discovery/interop layer. A coalition of this size organizing a standard that visibly excludes the two leading model labs signals the agent-interop layer is now a contested strategic asset, not settled infrastructure — building deeply against any single protocol today carries real switching-cost risk.

**Business Value:** (1) Do not standardize internal agent tooling exclusively on one discovery protocol (MCP, A2A, or ARD) until adoption signals are clearer — build an abstraction layer if committing to agent orchestration now. (2) Track which protocol GitHub Copilot Agent Finder and major SaaS vendors (Salesforce, ServiceNow) converge on in practice, since that will be the de facto standard for any company already using those platforms. (3) Treat this as a reason to favor vendor-neutral agent tooling over deep single-vendor agent-platform commitments for the next two quarters.

**Company Relevance:** 30 / 100 (higher for any team actively building multi-agent or tool-calling architectures)

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Observe.

**Expected Impact:** Medium-term — standards consolidation typically plays out over 6–12 months.

---

### Technology 5: AI Coding Trust Falls to 29% Even as Adoption Hits 92% — The Bottleneck Has Moved to Review

**Category:** Software Engineering / Developer Ecosystem

**What happened?**
Sonar's "State of Code" survey and corroborating data show AI coding-tool adoption reached 92% (up from 44% in 2023), with 75% of developers using AI for at least half their engineering work. But developer trust in AI-generated code fell to 29% (from over 70% in 2023), and developers now report spending more time reviewing AI output (11.4 hrs/week) than writing new code (9.8 hrs/week) — a reversal from 2024.

**Why it matters?**
The constraint on AI-assisted engineering velocity has shifted from "can we generate code fast enough" to "can we validate it fast enough." Any engineering organization still measuring AI ROI purely on adoption or lines-generated is measuring the wrong thing.

**Business Value:** (1) Redirect AI-tooling investment from generation capability (already saturated at 92% adoption) toward review, testing, and code-quality gating tooling — this is where the actual bottleneck now sits. (2) Reassess velocity expectations set when AI coding tools were adopted: if review time now exceeds writing time, headline "AI makes engineers N% faster" claims from vendors should be treated skeptically without accounting for review overhead. (3) Use this data point in any internal conversation about engineering headcount or timeline planning tied to AI-tooling adoption.

**Company Relevance:** 65 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Pilot (invest in AI-code review/quality tooling rather than further generation-tool rollout).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (changes tooling investment priority immediately); Medium-term (affects velocity/timeline assumptions).

---

### Technology 6: Usage-Based and Outcome-Based AI Pricing Becomes the Default — With Widespread "Bill Shock"

**Category:** Emerging Business Models / Enterprise Solutions

**What happened?**
Gartner forecasts 70% of businesses will prefer usage-based over per-seat pricing by 2026 as AI agents, not named users, do the work; roughly 40% of enterprise SaaS is expected to include outcome-based pricing, up from 15% two years ago. Concrete examples: Salesforce's Agentforce hit $800M ARR (29,000 deals in one quarter alone) and Intercom's Fin charges $0.99 only for a fully resolved support ticket. The countervailing data point: 78% of IT leaders report unexpected charges from consumption-based AI pricing, and 90% of CIOs cite cost forecasting as their top AI-deployment challenge.

**Why it matters?**
This is a direct, near-term budgeting risk, not a distant trend. Vendor contracts are actively shifting toward metered pricing faster than most finance/procurement processes have adapted forecasting tools for it.

**Business Value:** (1) Before signing or renewing any AI-vendor contract this year, explicitly negotiate spend caps, usage alerts, and forecasting visibility — do not accept usage-based terms without them, given the 78% bill-shock rate reported industry-wide. (2) Audit current AI-vendor contracts for hidden consumption-based components that may already be under-forecasted. (3) If evaluating outcome-based pricing (pay-per-resolved-ticket style) from a vendor, model worst-case volume scenarios before committing — the pricing structure shifts risk onto the buyer in ways per-seat pricing did not.

**Company Relevance:** 60 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Adopt (build spend-cap and forecasting requirements into all AI-vendor procurement immediately).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (contract terms); Long-term (structural shift in how AI spend is budgeted).

---

### Technology 7: Vertical AI and Alternative Inference Infrastructure Attract Mega-Rounds

**Category:** Startup Ecosystem / Cloud & Infrastructure

**What happened?**
Fireworks AI raised $1.505B (Series D, $17.5B valuation) explicitly positioning itself as an enterprise alternative to relying solely on frontier labs for model serving/fine-tuning, now serving 40 trillion tokens/day and $1B+ ARR (5x YoY), with customers including Shopify and Revolut. In the same week, Chai Discovery raised $400M ($3.8B valuation, nearly tripling in seven months) for AI-driven drug design, already used by Eli Lilly, Pfizer, and Novartis. AI dev-tools funding overall hit $2.0B YTD 2026 (+85% YoY), concentrated almost entirely in agentic coding platforms.

**Why it matters?**
Capital is validating both a "de-risk frontier-model dependency" thesis (Fireworks) and vertical-specific AI application layers (Chai Discovery) simultaneously — a signal that the market no longer sees "buy from OpenAI/Anthropic/Google directly" as the only viable AI-infrastructure strategy.

**Business Value:** (1) Evaluate whether a model-serving/customization layer (Fireworks-style) reduces total cost or lock-in risk for any internal AI workload currently calling frontier-lab APIs directly. (2) If operating in a regulated or specialized vertical, monitor whether domain-specific model vendors are emerging as a credible alternative to generic frontier models for that use case. (3) Treat the dev-tools funding concentration (agentic coding platforms capturing the overwhelming majority of capital) as a signal of which vendor category is most likely to still be well-funded and supported in 12–18 months versus which may consolidate or shut down.

**Company Relevance:** 40 / 100

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Observe; Pilot if current frontier-model API costs are a material line item.

**Expected Impact:** Medium-term.

# 3. New Tools & Platforms

---

### Tool: HAMi (GPU Virtualization for Kubernetes)

**What does it do?** Open-source middleware, newly accepted as a CNCF Incubating project, that lets platform teams slice a physical GPU by memory/core/device and schedule AI workloads across shared clusters with binpack/spread/topology-aware policies.

**Who should use it?** Platform/infrastructure engineering teams running shared GPU clusters for internal AI or ML workloads.

**Business Use Case:** Reduce GPU idle time and avoid proprietary GPU-fractioning vendor lock-in for any internal training/inference infrastructure.

**Estimated ROI:** Medium — meaningful for teams already running non-trivial shared GPU capacity; negligible if all AI workloads are outsourced to API vendors.

**Recommended Experiment:** Deploy HAMi in a non-production Kubernetes cluster and measure GPU utilization before/after for one internal AI workload.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1–2 weeks.

---

### Tool: Vercel Services

**What does it do?** Extends Vercel to first-class microservices — deploy frontend, backend, and services (Go, Rails, FastAPI, Docker-based) together in one project with private service-to-service routing and a single preview URL per commit.

**Who should use it?** Product engineering teams already on Vercel who currently host backend services separately.

**Business Use Case:** Consolidate infrastructure vendors and simplify preview/staging environments for full-stack feature development.

**Estimated ROI:** Medium — primarily an operational simplification/cost-consolidation play, not a new capability.

**Recommended Experiment:** Migrate one non-critical backend service into a Vercel Services project and compare deployment/preview workflow against the current setup.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1 week.

---

### Tool: Google Gemini Notebook (rebranded NotebookLM, now with code execution)

**What does it do?** Google's document/research assistant (30M+ users), rebranded under the Gemini product line, now with in-notebook secure code execution for deeper data analysis on uploaded sources.

**Who should use it?** Teams doing research synthesis, internal knowledge-base Q&A, or lightweight data analysis over internal documents.

**Business Use Case:** Internal research/knowledge work acceleration; potential replacement for ad hoc analysis currently done in spreadsheets or manual document review.

**Estimated ROI:** Low-Medium — incremental capability add for existing NotebookLM users; not a new adoption decision if already in the Google Workspace ecosystem.

**Recommended Experiment:** Have one team pilot the code-execution feature against a real internal dataset/document set and compare output quality to manual analysis.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 3–5 days.

---

### Tool: Cloudflare Precursor

**What does it do?** Continuous, session-long behavioral bot detection (mouse movement, typing cadence, clipboard activity) replacing static CAPTCHA-style checks, aimed specifically at distinguishing humans from increasingly sophisticated AI-agent bot traffic.

**Who should use it?** Any team operating customer-facing web properties already on Cloudflare, especially those seeing rising automated/agentic traffic.

**Business Use Case:** Defend against AI-agent-driven scraping, account abuse, and credential stuffing as agentic bot traffic grows (Cloudflare cites bot traffic now exceeding 57% of all web requests).

**Estimated ROI:** Medium-High for any public-facing product already experiencing bot abuse; low urgency otherwise.

**Recommended Experiment:** Enable Precursor in monitoring mode on one customer-facing property and compare detected bot traffic against current CAPTCHA-based numbers.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1 week.

---

# 4. Global Technology Direction

**Where is the market moving?**

Three simultaneous shifts define this window:

1. **AI capex is now a repricing force, not just a spending trend.** IBM's record stock crash and Apple's overtaking of Nvidia as the most valuable company both happened within a three-day span, and both reflect the same underlying question: is AI infrastructure spending converting to revenue, or is capital being reallocated faster than returns can be proven? The Q2 earnings window (July 22–30) is the next real data point.

2. **The compute layer is becoming multipolar.** Meta poaching AWS's SVP of compute to potentially launch "Meta Compute," combined with Nvidia halving its approved Asian chip-buyer list for compliance reasons, signals that cloud/compute supply is being actively restructured — both by new entrants and by tightening export-compliance regimes — not simply scaling up under the current hyperscaler structure.

3. **"AI agent" has become a first-class category for security, standards, and regulation simultaneously.** A rival agent-discovery standard (ARD), a new class of infostealer targeting AI-assistant sessions specifically, China's agent-specific regulatory regime, and Illinois's frontier-audit law all landed within the same ten-day window. This is not four unrelated stories — it is the market treating autonomous agents as an infrastructure category requiring its own governance, the same way cloud computing did a decade ago.

**What This Means For Our Company**

Budget and vendor decisions made this quarter should assume vendor pricing and terms are more negotiable than they were six months ago (IBM's warning is leverage), but also that AI-vendor bills are more likely to surprise (usage-based pricing, 78% bill-shock rate) — both point toward the same procurement discipline: negotiate caps and forecasting visibility now. On the technical side, any deepening investment in multi-agent tooling should stay protocol-agnostic until the ARD/MCP/A2A question settles, and any AI-assistant usage in engineering should now be paired with the same credential hygiene applied to cloud API keys.

# 6. CTO Decision Board

**START THIS WEEK**
1. Verify SonicWall SMA1000 and AD FS patch status; treat as potentially compromised if unpatched during the exposure window.
2. Audit npm dependencies and developer/CI environments for the Jscrambler and Miasma infostealer campaigns; rotate any exposed AI-assistant session tokens.
3. Add spend-cap and usage-visibility requirements to any AI-vendor contract currently under negotiation or renewal.

**EVALUATE THIS MONTH**
1. Pilot an AI-code-review/quality-gate tool to address the reversal in AI coding trust and review-time overhead.
2. Assess current frontier-model API spend against an alternative model-serving vendor (Fireworks-style) for cost/lock-in reduction.
3. Track which agent-discovery protocol (ARD, MCP, A2A) major SaaS vendors already in use (Salesforce, GitHub, ServiceNow) converge on before further agent-tooling investment.

**IGNORE FOR NOW**
1. Consumer/enterprise BCI and smart-glasses hardware (Neuracle implant, Samsung Galaxy Glasses) — 12–24 month horizon, no near-term action.
2. Humanoid robotics market moves (Agility Robotics SPAC, Walden Robotics) — relevant only to companies with physical logistics/manufacturing operations.
3. Post-quantum cryptography migration (Classic McEliece ISO standardization) — worth noting, not yet urgent; revisit when vendor/library support matures.

# 7. Trend Detection

**Trend:** AI capex is transitioning from a growth story to a scrutinized-ROI story.
**Confidence:** High
**Suggested Action:** Treat vendor pricing/terms as more negotiable through Q2 earnings season; do not lock into long-term infrastructure commitments until the July 22–30 earnings window clarifies whether capex is converting to revenue.

**Trend:** AI agents are consolidating into a distinct security, standards, and regulatory category — not a subset of general AI or software risk.
**Confidence:** High
**Suggested Action:** Stand up (or continue building, per yesterday's brief) a dedicated review process for AI-agent tooling covering security, credential handling, protocol choice, and regulatory exposure as one workstream, not scattered across existing categories.

**Trend:** Vertical and infrastructure-alternative AI plays are attracting concentrated mega-round capital, signaling reduced tolerance for frontier-model-only strategies market-wide.
**Confidence:** Medium-High
**Suggested Action:** Revisit build-vs-buy assumptions for any AI workload with significant frontier-API spend within the next quarter.

**Trend:** The AI coding productivity narrative is maturing from "adoption" to "trust and review overhead" as the binding constraint.
**Confidence:** High
**Suggested Action:** Shift engineering AI-tooling budget toward review/quality tooling rather than further generation-tool rollout.

# 8. Strategic Roadmap

**THIS MONTH**
- Patch/verify SonicWall SMA1000, AD FS, and SAP NetWeaver (CVE-2026-44747) if any are in the stack.
- Audit CI/CD and developer environments for npm-based AI-assistant credential theft; rotate exposed tokens.
- Build spend-cap and forecasting clauses into all AI-vendor contract renewals.

**NEXT 90 DAYS**
- Pilot an AI-code-review/quality-gate tool and re-baseline engineering velocity assumptions accordingly.
- Evaluate an alternative model-serving vendor for any workload with material frontier-API costs.
- Monitor Q2 hyperscaler earnings (July 22–30) for AI-capex-to-revenue signals before committing to new long-term infrastructure contracts.

**NEXT 12 MONTHS**
- Track agent-discovery protocol consolidation (ARD vs. MCP vs. A2A) before deepening multi-agent architecture investment.
- Build compliance readiness for AI-agent-specific regulation (Illinois audit requirements phase in 2027–2028; EU AI Act transparency deadline August 2, 2026) if operating in or selling into those jurisdictions.
- Reassess vendor contract structures company-wide as usage/outcome-based AI pricing becomes the market default.
